title: It's All About the Church
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman frames Free the States' lobbying work as secondary to its primary mission of stirring the Church of Jesus Christ to repent of abortion apathy and lead the fight to abolish abortion. He argues that even 'failed' abolition bills and campaigns are succeeding because they expose pro-life compromise and awaken Christians.
audiences: Pastor weighing whether to preach abolition from the pulpit
Donor evaluating Free the States' strategy and effectiveness
Lay churchgoer wondering what role the Church plays in ending abortion
Political strategist who measures success by bills passed and elections won

questions.question: If Free the States is a lobbying organization, why does it say its primary focus is not the legislature?
questions.audience: Donor evaluating Free the States' strategy and effectiveness
questions.answer: Silberman explicitly distinguishes the organization's tactics (lobbying, meeting officials, delivering memos) from its central mission, which is to provoke the Church of Jesus Christ to take up the cause of abolishing abortion. Every activity is treated as instrumental to that end, not as the end itself.
questions.quote: Our primary project is exhorting, equipping, and where necessary, rebuking the people of God. Our most important objective by far is helping the Church take its rightful place on the front lines of the battle to establish justice for the fatherless.

questions.question: What does Silberman point to as evidence that the Church is actually starting to embrace abolitionism?
questions.audience: Lay churchgoer wondering what role the Church plays in ending abortion
questions.answer: He cites Ekklesia Muskogee organizing an abolition conference on the American Church's need to repent for abortion apathy, pastors like Brett Baggett, Nate Yarbrough, and Brandon Allen along with their congregations embracing abolitionist doctrine, and dozens of Oklahoma pastors preaching abolition from the pulpit.
questions.quote: Accordingly, there is no greater encouragement to us than seeing the people of Ekklesia Muskogee organize and facilitate an amazing abolition conference about the American Church's need to repent for abortion apathy and compromise.

questions.question: How does Silberman answer the charge that Dan Fisher's run for governor and Joseph Silk's abolition bills failed?
questions.audience: Political strategist who measures success by bills passed and elections won
questions.answer: He rejects the framing of failure entirely, arguing that each abolitionist campaign and bill draws a clear line between abolitionism and the pro-life movement, forces pro-life leaders to expose themselves as anti-abolitionist, and incrementally awakens the Church. Success is measured by Christian conviction, not legislative tallies.
questions.quote: Every time a clear line is drawn between the Abolitionist Movement and the Pro-Life Movement, people's eyes are opened to a Biblical way of fighting abortion. Every time an abolitionist project is put forward, the pro-life leaders are smoked out into the open and forced to expose themselves as anti-abolitionists, and the church wakes up a little bit more.

questions.question: Why does Silberman believe the Church specifically (and not the legislature alone) has the power to end abortion?
questions.audience: Pastor weighing whether to preach abolition from the pulpit
questions.answer: He grounds the Church's power in the gospel itself, the most powerful truth in the universe, which the Spirit uses to convict and turn hearts. He invokes Francis Schaeffer's claim that every abortion clinic operates by the permission of the Church, implying that withdrawing that permission through faithful proclamation can shut them down.
questions.quote: Francis Schaeffer was correct in writing, 'Every abortion clinic should have a sign in front of it saying, Open by the permission of the Church.' Schaeffer is correct because the Church of Jesus Christ has the power to abolish abortion.

questions.question: What explanation does Silberman give for abortion remaining legal in heavily Christian states?
questions.answer: He locates the fault not in unbelievers or politicians but in professing Christians who refuse to bring their confessed truth into open conflict with the surrounding culture of death. The persistence of legal child sacrifice in such places, he argues, is itself an indictment of Christian complacency.
questions.quote: The only way child sacrifice can remain legal in a state where 80 percent of the population professes to believe that Truth is if those Christians are failing to bring it into conflict with the culture of death.

questions.question: What hopeful signs does Silberman close with that suggest abortion will actually be abolished?
questions.audience: Donor evaluating Free the States' strategy and effectiveness
questions.answer: He points to a righteous standard now being raised, bold Christians answering the call, and churches taking responsibility to rescue the perishing and establish justice for the fatherless. These visible fruits, in his view, are the basis for confidence that the movement is succeeding.
questions.quote: But the righteous standard has been raised. Bold Christians are answering the call. Churches are taking on the responsibility to rescue those being led to slaughter and establish justice for the fatherless.

related.title: Pastor Ben Brewer to Churches: Join us for Abolition Month!
related.reason: A concrete example of the kind of pastor-led church mobilization Silberman celebrates as Free the States' primary objective.

related.title: Pastor Sam Ketcher: Repent With Us and Come to Abolition Day
related.reason: Reinforces the theme of Church repentance for abortion apathy that drives this article.

related.title: What the Still, Small Voice Said: A Parable for Churches Who Won't Work For Abolition
related.reason: Directly addresses the complacent Church that Silberman says is permitting abortion clinics to operate.

related.title: SBC Pastors Publish 'Resolution on Abolishing Abortion' for 2021 Convention Meeting
related.reason: An institutional Church example of pastors moving toward the abolitionist posture this article calls for.

related.title: Todd Fisher Elected President of OK Baptists. A Step Toward or Away From Abolition?
related.reason: Tests Silberman's thesis on the Oklahoma Baptist establishment, the same Christian population whose silence he indicts here.

related.title: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
related.reason: Extends the call to Church repentance from this article into a self-examination of the abolitionist movement itself.

related.title: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
related.reason: Connects to Silberman's defense of the Dan Fisher campaign as a project that smoked out anti-abolitionist leaders.
